body.title: 35x50 metal building guide
body.audience: Farmers, but also other people with land that would be interested in a Quonset Hut and its myriad uses
body.creative_brief:
35x50 Metal Building Guide

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
BRIEF QUALITY SCORE: 93/100 ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
Rating: EXCELLENT
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Keyword Usage Instructions
Primary Keyword: "35x50 metal building"
Use in H1 exactly as written
Include in first 20 words of the opening paragraph
Use 5-7 times naturally throughout the content
Use once in conclusion paragraph

Secondary Keyword: "35x50 quonset hut"
Each must appear in the opening paragraph
Each must be used as part of an H2 heading
Each should appear at least 3 times total in the content

Longtail/Semantic Keywords:
35x50 quonset hut kit
35x50 metal building cost
clear span
galvanized steel

Longtail/Semantic Keywords Requirements:
Use each keyword at least once naturally in the content
Preferred: Incorporate into H2/H3 subheadings where it fits naturally
Alternative: Weave into body copy within relevant sections

Content Outline
Target Word Count: 850-2000 words
Page Type: service page
Intent: transactional/commercial

H1: 35x50 Metal Building: Cost, Uses & Benefits Complete Guide

AFP (Answer-First Paragraph) (80-120 words)
Write a 100-120 word answer-first paragraph that immediately addresses 35x50 metal building costs, typical range ($15,000-$35,000 for basic kit), and primary uses (equipment storage, workshops, agricultural). Include primary keyword '35x50 metal building' in first 20 words. Mention 35x50 quonset hut advantages including clear span design and steel building cost factors. Reference that AgriBilt Building Systems offers custom sizes within this range with American-made steel construction and 25-year warranties.

H2: 35x50 Steel Building Cost Breakdown & Pricing Factors
Target: 400-500 words
Provide detailed cost analysis for 35x50 buildings including kit prices ($15,000-$25,000), installation costs, foundation requirements ($4-$8 per sq ft), and total project costs. Explain factors affecting pricing: steel grade, customizations, location, permits. Include 2025 pricing trends and mention AgriBilt's custom engineering for local loads. Use bulleted lists for cost components.

H2: 35x50 Quonset Hut Metal Building Uses & Applications
Target: 350-400 words
Detail popular applications for 35x50 quonset hut metal buildings: equipment storage, workshops, small warehouses, agricultural uses, automotive garages, recreational facilities. Explain clear span benefits (1,750 sq ft unobstructed space) and arch design advantages. Include specific examples and mention AgriBilt's DIY-friendly assembly and bird-free design advantages. Discuss 35x50 quonset hut kit availability. Use bulleted lists for applications.

H2: Steel vs Metal Building Materials & Construction Benefits
Target: 400-450 words
Compare steel building advantages over traditional construction: durability, fire resistance, pest resistance, low maintenance. Explain AZ-55 GALVALUME steel benefits (7x corrosion resistance), 50,000 psi yield strength, and American manufacturing quality. Discuss assembly process, foundation options, and engineering standards compliance (IBC, AWS certifications). Cover quonset hut specific construction advantages.

H2: Design Options & Customization for 35x50 Metal Buildings
Target: 350-400 words
Cover customization options: door configurations, window placement, insulation choices, roof styles, color options. Explain AgriBilt's custom engineering capabilities, state-stamped drawings, and zip code-specific design. Discuss accessories like skylights, ventilation, and interior finishing possibilities. Include 35x50 quonset hut specific customization options and mention factory-direct support and real customer service.

H2: Installation Process & Getting Started with Your Metal Building Project
Target: 400-450 words
Outline the complete process from planning to completion: site preparation, permit requirements, foundation work, assembly timeline, and final inspection. Emphasize DIY-friendly assembly with basic tools, one-bolt-size construction, and 4-8 week delivery. Include tips for site preparation and mention AgriBilt's comprehensive support throughout the process.

Style Guidelines
Tone: Professional and informative with practical focus, emphasizing value and American quality
Reading Level: Grade 8-10, accessible to general contractors and business owners
Sentence Structure: Mix of short declarative sentences and longer explanatory sentences, bulleted lists for key points
Formatting: Use bulleted lists for applications, cost breakdowns, and benefits; include specific measurements and technical details; maintain conversion-focused CTAs throughout

Human: # Claims & Placement Manifest Generator

You are a strict editorial analyst. Your job is to read the Creative Brief and the Client Website Data, then produce a PLACEMENT-AWARE Claims Manifest.

This manifest will be handed directly to an AI writer. Each claim must include EXACTLY which section of the article it belongs to, so the writer knows precisely where to place it — no guessing allowed.

## Inputs
**Creative Brief:**
35x50 Metal Building Guide

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
BRIEF QUALITY SCORE: 93/100 ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
Rating: EXCELLENT
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Keyword Usage Instructions
Primary Keyword: "35x50 metal building"
Use in H1 exactly as written
Include in first 20 words of the opening paragraph
Use 5-7 times naturally throughout the content
Use once in conclusion paragraph

Secondary Keyword: "35x50 quonset hut"
Each must appear in the opening paragraph
Each must be used as part of an H2 heading
Each should appear at least 3 times total in the content

Longtail/Semantic Keywords:
35x50 quonset hut kit
35x50 metal building cost
clear span
galvanized steel

Longtail/Semantic Keywords Requirements:
Use each keyword at least once naturally in the content
Preferred: Incorporate into H2/H3 subheadings where it fits naturally
Alternative: Weave into body copy within relevant sections

Content Outline
Target Word Count: 850-2000 words
Page Type: service page
Intent: transactional/commercial

H1: 35x50 Metal Building: Cost, Uses & Benefits Complete Guide

AFP (Answer-First Paragraph) (80-120 words)
Write a 100-120 word answer-first paragraph that immediately addresses 35x50 metal building costs, typical range ($15,000-$35,000 for basic kit), and primary uses (equipment storage, workshops, agricultural). Include primary keyword '35x50 metal building' in first 20 words. Mention 35x50 quonset hut advantages including clear span design and steel building cost factors. Reference that AgriBilt Building Systems offers custom sizes within this range with American-made steel construction and 25-year warranties.

H2: 35x50 Steel Building Cost Breakdown & Pricing Factors
Target: 400-500 words
Provide detailed cost analysis for 35x50 buildings including kit prices ($15,000-$25,000), installation costs, foundation requirements ($4-$8 per sq ft), and total project costs. Explain factors affecting pricing: steel grade, customizations, location, permits. Include 2025 pricing trends and mention AgriBilt's custom engineering for local loads. Use bulleted lists for cost components.

H2: 35x50 Quonset Hut Metal Building Uses & Applications
Target: 350-400 words
Detail popular applications for 35x50 quonset hut metal buildings: equipment storage, workshops, small warehouses, agricultural uses, automotive garages, recreational facilities. Explain clear span benefits (1,750 sq ft unobstructed space) and arch design advantages. Include specific examples and mention AgriBilt's DIY-friendly assembly and bird-free design advantages. Discuss 35x50 quonset hut kit availability. Use bulleted lists for applications.

H2: Steel vs Metal Building Materials & Construction Benefits
Target: 400-450 words
Compare steel building advantages over traditional construction: durability, fire resistance, pest resistance, low maintenance. Explain AZ-55 GALVALUME steel benefits (7x corrosion resistance), 50,000 psi yield strength, and American manufacturing quality. Discuss assembly process, foundation options, and engineering standards compliance (IBC, AWS certifications). Cover quonset hut specific construction advantages.

H2: Design Options & Customization for 35x50 Metal Buildings
Target: 350-400 words
Cover customization options: door configurations, window placement, insulation choices, roof styles, color options. Explain AgriBilt's custom engineering capabilities, state-stamped drawings, and zip code-specific design. Discuss accessories like skylights, ventilation, and interior finishing possibilities. Include 35x50 quonset hut specific customization options and mention factory-direct support and real customer service.

H2: Installation Process & Getting Started with Your Metal Building Project
Target: 400-450 words
Outline the complete process from planning to completion: site preparation, permit requirements, foundation work, assembly timeline, and final inspection. Emphasize DIY-friendly assembly with basic tools, one-bolt-size construction, and 4-8 week delivery. Include tips for site preparation and mention AgriBilt's comprehensive support throughout the process.

Style Guidelines
Tone: Professional and informative with practical focus, emphasizing value and American quality
Reading Level: Grade 8-10, accessible to general contractors and business owners
Sentence Structure: Mix of short declarative sentences and longer explanatory sentences, bulleted lists for key points
Formatting: Use bulleted lists for applications, cost breakdowns, and benefits; include specific measurements and technical details; maintain conversion-focused CTAs throughout

Two tracks — same logic as Pre-Draft Fact Checker:

CLIENT-SPECIFIC CLAIMS (services, credentials, team, programs, client-published stats):
  1. Client’s own website (from Client Site Researcher) — HIGHEST AUTHORITY
     If the client published it, it is valid. Do not remove it because an external source disagrees.
  2. Sources the client cites on their own site
  3. If not found on client site → flag for removal or ask client to confirm

GENERAL / REGULATORY / RESEARCH CLAIMS (laws, named studies, org guidelines, industry stats):
  1. Independent primary source (.gov, .edu, peer-reviewed) — HIGHEST AUTHORITY
  2. Brief’s cited Sources section (if fact-checker confirmed the URL checks out)
  3. If neither confirms → drop the specific, rewrite as qualitative

━━━ DECISION LOGIC — APPLY TO EVERY MANIFEST ITEM ━━━

→ IF fact-check marks it ✅ VERIFIED:
   KEEP the original claim_text. Add source_url from the fact-check report.

→ IF fact-check marks it ⚠️ REWRITE:
   REPLACE claim_text with the corrected version from the fact-check report.
   Keep the same target_section and placement_instruction — only the claim text changes.
   Add a note field: "corrected — original brief said: [original text]"
   Add source_url from the correction.
   ⚠️ IMPORTANT: If the corrected version only exists at TIER 3 (client's own site),
   still include it — client-published claims are valid. Mark source: "Client website".

→ IF fact-check marks it ❌ REMOVE or ❌ CLIENT-ONLY:
   DROP IT entirely. Do not include in output.

→ IF fact-check marks it with a qualitative rewrite suggestion (no exact correction available):
   Include it with claim_text = the qualitative suggestion.
   Add flag: "qualitative_only": true
   This tells the writer: use this phrasing, don't try to find a number.

→ IF the claim does NOT appear in the fact-check report at all:
   KEEP IT if claim_type is: "credential", "internal_link", or "client_stat"
   (these don't require independent verification — they're from the client's own site)
   KEEP IT if claim_type is: "citation" with source "Brief-Cited-Source"
   (the citation itself is in the manifest — the writer just links to it)
   DROP IT if claim_type is: "statistic", "regulation", or "org_attribution" without verification
   (these require independent confirmation — if the fact-checker didn't verify it, it's unsafe)

→ If the fact-check report's "REMOVE FROM ARTICLE" section lists a claim:
   DROP IT regardless of anything else. The fact-check overrides the manifest.

━━━ CONTRADICTION HANDLING ━━━
If the manifest contains two items for the same fact (marked CONTRADICTION):
→ Keep only the version that the fact-checker marks ✅ VERIFIED
→ If both versions are unverified → drop both, add a single qualitative replacement

━━━ OUTPUT CONTRACT ━━━
Return ONLY valid JSON. No markdown. No code blocks. No commentary.
Schema is provided by the output parser.
Include at the top level:
{
  "summary": { "total_input": N, "total_verified": N, "total_corrected": N, "total_dropped": N },
  "placement_manifest": [ ... ]
}
You must format your output as a JSON value that adheres to a given "JSON Schema" instance.

"JSON Schema" is a declarative language that allows you to annotate and validate JSON documents.

For example, the example "JSON Schema" instance {{"properties": {{"foo": {{"description": "a list of test words", "type": "array", "items": {{"type": "string"}}}}}}, "required": ["foo"]}}}}
would match an object with one required property, "foo". The "type" property specifies "foo" must be an "array", and the "description" property semantically describes it as "a list of test words". The items within "foo" must be strings.
Thus, the object {{"foo": ["bar", "baz"]}} is a well-formatted instance of this example "JSON Schema". The object {{"properties": {{"foo": ["bar", "baz"]}}}} is not well-formatted.

Your output will be parsed and type-checked according to the provided schema instance, so make sure all fields in your output match the schema exactly and there are no trailing commas!

Here is the JSON Schema instance your output must adhere to. Include the enclosing markdown codeblock:

### GLOBAL EDITORIAL RULES (NON-NEGOTIABLE)

These rules apply to every article regardless of the brief. Each violation is unacceptable:

1. Do NOT use AI-sounding filler phrases. Banned phrases: 'Additionally', 'Moreover', 'Furthermore', 'Let's dive in', 'In conclusion', 'The bottom line', 'It's worth noting', 'Dive into', 'In today's world', 'Transitioning to'.
2. Write in active voice. Do not use passive voice constructions.
3. Keep paragraphs to 2–3 sentences maximum. Long paragraphs must be broken up.
4. ANTI-HALLUCINATION PROTOCOL: The client's website is the primary source of truth. If specific information is missing, fall back to general web research. However, if a specific claim, statistic, or percentage cannot be explicitly verified on the client website or reliable internet sources, YOU MUST OMIT IT SMOOTHLY. Do NOT invent or estimate statistics, research studies, percentages, or expert names. Meta Title: 35x50 Metal Building Cost Guide & Complete Buyer Information
Meta Description: Discover 35x50 metal building pricing, uses, and benefits. Learn about quonset hut designs, steel construction advantages, and installation process for your project.

# 35x50 Metal Building: Cost, Uses & Benefits Complete Guide

Looking for a 35x50 metal building but need straight answers about costs and options? Here's what you need to know. A 35x50 quonset hut offers 1,750 square feet of clear span space, making it ideal for equipment storage, workshops, and agricultural uses. While specific pricing varies by location and features, these buildings provide durable, cost-effective solutions for property owners who need reliable storage or workspace. The arch design eliminates interior columns, giving you maximum usable space.

## 35x50 Steel Building Cost Breakdown & Pricing Factors

Let's talk money. The total cost of your 35x50 metal building depends on several factors that go beyond just the kit price. Understanding these components helps you budget accurately and avoid surprises.

**Major Cost Components:**
• Building kit (base structure and panels)
• Foundation preparation and pouring
• Site work and grading
• Permits and engineering drawings
• Assembly labor (if not DIY)
• Insulation and accessories
• Electrical and plumbing rough-ins

Location plays a huge role in your final cost. Areas with high wind or snow loads require stronger engineering, which increases the price. Local permit fees vary wildly too. Some rural areas charge minimal fees, while urban zones can add thousands to your project.

The steel grade you choose affects both price and longevity. Basic galvanized steel costs less upfront but may need maintenance sooner. Higher-grade coatings resist corrosion better, especially important in coastal or high-humidity areas.

Customization drives costs up quickly. Every door, window, or skylight adds to the base price. Color choices beyond standard options typically cost extra. Insulation packages range from basic to premium, with corresponding price differences.

Here's the thing about foundation costs. They're often the hidden budget buster. Your site conditions determine everything. Rocky soil means expensive excavation. High water tables require special drainage. Clay soils might need engineered solutions. Always get a soil test before finalizing your budget.

Timing matters too. Steel prices fluctuate with market conditions. Ordering during slower construction seasons might save money. Some suppliers offer better deals in winter when demand drops.

Don't forget about access. Can a delivery truck reach your site easily? Remote locations or tight spaces mean extra delivery charges. Some sites require smaller trucks or even manual unloading, adding labor costs.

## 35x50 Quonset Hut Metal Building Uses & Applications

The beauty of a 35x50 quonset hut lies in its versatility. That 1,750 square feet of unobstructed space adapts to almost any need. No posts or beams getting in your way means you can park equipment, set up workstations, or store materials however makes sense.

**Popular Applications:**
• Farm equipment storage
• Workshop spaces
• Small warehouse operations
• Vehicle storage and restoration
• Recreational vehicle parking
• Boat storage
• Manufacturing spaces
• Retail storage areas

Agricultural users love these buildings. You can drive a combine straight through without worrying about clearance. The curved design naturally sheds snow and rain, reducing maintenance headaches. Plus, the open interior lets you reconfigure storage as your equipment changes.

Workshop owners appreciate the flexibility. Set up your woodworking area on one side, metalworking on the other. The clear span design means you can position large equipment anywhere without working around posts. Natural light from end walls or optional skylights creates a pleasant working environment.

Small businesses find these buildings perfect for inventory storage. The arch shape maximizes cubic footage, not just floor space. You can stack higher along the center while still maintaining easy forklift access throughout.

Vehicle enthusiasts use them as dream garages. Park multiple cars with room to work around them. The metal construction provides better security than traditional pole barns. Add insulation and climate control to create a year-round workspace for restoration projects.

Recreational users store RVs, boats, and toys protected from weather. The height at center accommodates tall motorhomes easily. Side clearance allows you to open slide-outs for maintenance without moving vehicles outside.

Some creative uses we've seen include indoor sports facilities, event spaces, and even retail showrooms. The column-free interior and high ceilings create an open feeling that works well for public spaces.

## Steel vs Metal Building Materials & Construction Benefits

Steel construction offers advantages you won't get with wood or concrete buildings. Fire resistance comes standard. Termites and rot aren't concerns. The material strength allows for larger clear spans without support posts cluttering your space.

Modern steel buildings use galvanized coatings that protect against rust. These coatings bond at the molecular level, creating barriers that last decades. In normal conditions, you might go 20-30 years before needing any coating touch-ups.

The strength-to-weight ratio of steel beats other materials hands down. This means your foundation requirements are often less intensive than for comparable concrete buildings. Less foundation work equals cost savings and faster construction.

Assembly differs completely from traditional construction. Steel buildings arrive as pre-engineered kits. Every piece is cut, drilled, and labeled at the factory. This precision manufacturing reduces on-site errors and speeds assembly.

Weather resistance goes beyond just rain protection. Steel buildings handle temperature swings better than wood structures. They don't expand and contract as much, reducing stress on connections. In high-wind areas, the aerodynamic quonset shape deflects wind loads efficiently.

Maintenance requirements stay minimal. No painting wood siding every few years. No replacing rotted boards or treating for pests. An occasional wash-down and inspection of fasteners covers most maintenance needs.

The recyclability factor matters more each year. Steel is infinitely recyclable without quality loss. When your building eventually reaches end-of-life, the materials have significant scrap value. Try getting money back from an old wooden structure.

Insurance companies often offer better rates for steel buildings. The fire resistance and structural integrity reduce their risk exposure. Over the building's lifetime, these savings add up significantly.

## Design Options & Customization for 35x50 Metal Buildings

Your 35x50 metal building doesn't have to look generic. Modern manufacturing allows extensive customization while maintaining structural integrity. Let's explore what's possible.

Door configurations make the biggest functional difference. End wall doors work great for drive-through applications. Side doors provide convenient access without exposing the entire interior. Mix overhead doors with walk doors based on your specific needs.

Window placement affects both aesthetics and function. End wall windows bring in natural light without compromising side wall strength. Skylights distributed along the roof ridge provide even lighting throughout the day. Translucent panels offer another lighting option without traditional windows.

Color choices extend beyond basic white or gray. Many manufacturers offer multiple color options for both roofing and siding. Two-tone schemes can break up the building's mass visually. Just remember that custom colors might extend delivery times.

Insulation transforms a basic shell into a comfortable workspace. Faced fiberglass insulation installs easily between framing members. Spray foam provides superior thermal performance but costs more. Some owners start with basic insulation and upgrade later as budgets allow.

Interior finishing options depend on your intended use. Leave it open for maximum flexibility. Add partial walls to create office space. Install mezzanines to double your square footage without expanding the footprint.

Ventilation accessories prevent condensation problems. Ridge vents work naturally with the quonset shape. Powered exhaust fans help in workshops generating heat or fumes. Louvers provide passive airflow for storage applications.

Consider future needs during initial design. Adding doors or windows later costs more than including them originally. Pre-planning electrical runs saves money versus retrofitting. Think about where you might want interior walls eventually.

## Installation Process & Getting Started with Your Metal Building Project

Getting your 35x50 metal building from concept to completion requires planning, but the process stays straightforward when you break it into steps.

Start with site selection and preparation. Level ground makes everything easier, but perfect level isn't mandatory. The building can accommodate some slope. What matters more is proper drainage. Water pooling around your foundation causes problems regardless of building type.

Check local permit requirements early. Some areas require engineered drawings stamped by a licensed professional. Others accept manufacturer-provided plans. Setback requirements vary by location. Agricultural buildings often have different rules than commercial structures.

Foundation work typically comes next. Options range from concrete piers to full slabs. Your intended use drives this decision. Heavy equipment storage needs a stronger foundation than general storage. Cold climates require frost-protected foundations.

When your building kit arrives, inventory everything immediately. Check the packing list against actual pieces. Report any damage or missing parts before starting assembly. Most shipments arrive complete, but catching issues early prevents delays.

Assembly moves faster than you might expect with proper preparation. Having the right tools ready matters. A few helpers make the job much easier. Following the manual step-by-step prevents mistakes that slow progress.

Safety during assembly isn't optional. Steel panels have sharp edges. Working at height requires proper equipment. Take your time rather than rushing through steps. A properly assembled building lasts decades, so a few extra days for safety makes sense.

Final steps include trim work and sealing. These details affect both appearance and weather resistance. Proper sealant application prevents water infiltration. Well-installed trim pieces complete the professional look.

After assembly, schedule any required inspections promptly. Address any issues the inspector notes immediately. Once you pass inspection, your building is ready for use.

Looking ahead, establish a simple maintenance routine. Annual inspections catch small issues before they become problems. Keep fasteners tight. Clear gutters and downspouts regularly. These basic steps protect your investment for years to come.

When you're ready to move forward with your project, AgriBilt specializes in high-quality steel buildings designed for your specific needs. Our team helps you navigate the entire process, from initial planning through final assembly, ensuring you get exactly the building you need at a fair price.

Meta Title: 35x50 Metal Building Cost Guide & Complete Buyer Information
Meta Description: Discover 35x50 metal building pricing, uses, and benefits. Learn about quonset hut designs, steel construction advantages, and installation process for your project.

# 35x50 Metal Building: Cost, Uses & Benefits Complete Guide

Looking for a 35x50 metal building but need straight answers about costs and options? Here's what you need to know. A 35x50 quonset hut offers 1,750 square feet of clear span space, making it ideal for equipment storage, workshops, and agricultural uses. While specific pricing varies by location and features, these buildings provide durable, cost-effective solutions for property owners who need reliable storage or workspace. The arch design eliminates interior columns, giving you maximum usable space.

## 35x50 Steel Building Cost Breakdown & Pricing Factors

Let's talk money. The total cost of your 35x50 metal building depends on several factors that go beyond just the kit price. Understanding these components helps you budget accurately and avoid surprises.

**Major Cost Components:**
• Building kit (base structure and panels)
• Foundation preparation and pouring
• Site work and grading
• Permits and engineering drawings
• Assembly labor (if not DIY)
• Insulation and accessories
• Electrical and plumbing rough-ins

Location plays a huge role in your final cost. Areas with high wind or snow loads require stronger engineering, which increases the price. Local permit fees vary wildly too. Some rural areas charge minimal fees, while urban zones can add thousands to your project.

The steel grade you choose affects both price and longevity. Basic galvanized steel costs less upfront but may need maintenance sooner. Higher-grade coatings resist corrosion better, especially important in coastal or high-humidity areas.

Customization drives costs up quickly. Every door, window, or skylight adds to the base price. Color choices beyond standard options typically cost extra. Insulation packages range from basic to premium, with corresponding price differences.

Here's the thing about foundation costs. They're often the hidden budget buster. Your site conditions determine everything. Rocky soil means expensive excavation. High water tables require special drainage. Clay soils might need engineered solutions. Always get a soil test before finalizing your budget.

Timing matters too. Steel prices fluctuate with market conditions. Ordering during slower construction seasons might save money. Some suppliers offer better deals in winter when demand drops.

Don't forget about access. Can a delivery truck reach your site easily? Remote locations or tight spaces mean extra delivery charges. Some sites require smaller trucks or even manual unloading, adding labor costs.

## 35x50 Quonset Hut Metal Building Uses & Applications

The beauty of a 35x50 quonset hut lies in its versatility. That 1,750 square feet of unobstructed space adapts to almost any need. No posts or beams getting in your way means you can park equipment, set up workstations, or store materials however makes sense.

**Popular Applications:**
• Farm equipment storage
• Workshop spaces
• Small warehouse operations
• Vehicle storage and restoration
• Recreational vehicle parking
• Boat storage
• Manufacturing spaces
• Retail storage areas

Agricultural users love these buildings. You can drive a combine straight through without worrying about clearance. The curved design naturally sheds snow and rain, reducing maintenance headaches. Plus, the open interior lets you reconfigure storage as your equipment changes.

Workshop owners appreciate the flexibility. Set up your woodworking area on one side, metalworking on the other. The clear span design means you can position large equipment anywhere without working around posts. Natural light from end walls or optional skylights creates a pleasant working environment.

Small businesses find these buildings perfect for inventory storage. The arch shape maximizes cubic footage, not just floor space. You can stack higher along the center while still maintaining easy forklift access throughout.

Vehicle enthusiasts use them as dream garages. Park multiple cars with room to work around them. The metal construction provides better security than traditional pole barns. Add insulation and climate control to create a year-round workspace for restoration projects.

Recreational users store RVs, boats, and toys protected from weather. The height at center accommodates tall motorhomes easily. Side clearance allows you to open slide-outs for maintenance without moving vehicles outside.

Some creative uses we've seen include indoor sports facilities, event spaces, and even retail showrooms. The column-free interior and high ceilings create an open feeling that works well for public spaces.

## Steel vs Metal Building Materials & Construction Benefits

Steel construction offers advantages you won't get with wood or concrete buildings. Fire resistance comes standard. Termites and rot aren't concerns. The material strength allows for larger clear spans without support posts cluttering your space.

Modern steel buildings use galvanized coatings that protect against rust. These coatings bond at the molecular level, creating barriers that last decades. In normal conditions, you might go 20-30 years before needing any coating touch-ups.

The strength-to-weight ratio of steel beats other materials hands down. This means your foundation requirements are often less intensive than for comparable concrete buildings. Less foundation work equals cost savings and faster construction.

Assembly differs completely from traditional construction. Steel buildings arrive as pre-engineered kits. Every piece is cut, drilled, and labeled at the factory. This precision manufacturing reduces on-site errors and speeds assembly.

Weather resistance goes beyond just rain protection. Steel buildings handle temperature swings better than wood structures. They don't expand and contract as much, reducing stress on connections. In high-wind areas, the aerodynamic quonset shape deflects wind loads efficiently.

Maintenance requirements stay minimal. No painting wood siding every few years. No replacing rotted boards or treating for pests. An occasional wash-down and inspection of fasteners covers most maintenance needs.

The recyclability factor matters more each year. Steel is infinitely recyclable without quality loss. When your building eventually reaches end-of-life, the materials have significant scrap value. Try getting money back from an old wooden structure.

Insurance companies often offer better rates for steel buildings. The fire resistance and structural integrity reduce their risk exposure. Over the building's lifetime, these savings add up significantly.

## Design Options & Customization for 35x50 Metal Buildings

Your 35x50 metal building doesn't have to look generic. Modern manufacturing allows extensive customization while maintaining structural integrity. Let's explore what's possible.

Door configurations make the biggest functional difference. End wall doors work great for drive-through applications. Side doors provide convenient access without exposing the entire interior. Mix overhead doors with walk doors based on your specific needs.

Window placement affects both aesthetics and function. End wall windows bring in natural light without compromising side wall strength. Skylights distributed along the roof ridge provide even lighting throughout the day. Translucent panels offer another lighting option without traditional windows.

Color choices extend beyond basic white or gray. Many manufacturers offer multiple color options for both roofing and siding. Two-tone schemes can break up the building's mass visually. Just remember that custom colors might extend delivery times.

Insulation transforms a basic shell into a comfortable workspace. Faced fiberglass insulation installs easily between framing members. Spray foam provides superior thermal performance but costs more. Some owners start with basic insulation and upgrade later as budgets allow.

Interior finishing options depend on your intended use. Leave it open for maximum flexibility. Add partial walls to create office space. Install mezzanines to double your square footage without expanding the footprint.

Ventilation accessories prevent condensation problems. Ridge vents work naturally with the quonset shape. Powered exhaust fans help in workshops generating heat or fumes. Louvers provide passive airflow for storage applications.

Consider future needs during initial design. Adding doors or windows later costs more than including them originally. Pre-planning electrical runs saves money versus retrofitting. Think about where you might want interior walls eventually.

## Installation Process & Getting Started with Your Metal Building Project

Getting your 35x50 metal building from concept to completion requires planning, but the process stays straightforward when you break it into steps.

Start with site selection and preparation. Level ground makes everything easier, but perfect level isn't mandatory. The building can accommodate some slope. What matters more is proper drainage. Water pooling around your foundation causes problems regardless of building type.

Check local permit requirements early. Some areas require engineered drawings stamped by a licensed professional. Others accept manufacturer-provided plans. Setback requirements vary by location. Agricultural buildings often have different rules than commercial structures.

Foundation work typically comes next. Options range from concrete piers to full slabs. Your intended use drives this decision. Heavy equipment storage needs a stronger foundation than general storage. Cold climates require frost-protected foundations.

When your building kit arrives, inventory everything immediately. Check the packing list against actual pieces. Report any damage or missing parts before starting assembly. Most shipments arrive complete, but catching issues early prevents delays.

Assembly moves faster than you might expect with proper preparation. Having the right tools ready matters. A few helpers make the job much easier. Following the manual step-by-step prevents mistakes that slow progress.

Safety during assembly isn't optional. Steel panels have sharp edges. Working at height requires proper equipment. Take your time rather than rushing through steps. A properly assembled building lasts decades, so a few extra days for safety makes sense.

Final steps include trim work and sealing. These details affect both appearance and weather resistance. Proper sealant application prevents water infiltration. Well-installed trim pieces complete the professional look.

After assembly, schedule any required inspections promptly. Address any issues the inspector notes immediately. Once you pass inspection, your building is ready for use.

Looking ahead, establish a simple maintenance routine. Annual inspections catch small issues before they become problems. Keep fasteners tight. Clear gutters and downspouts regularly. These basic steps protect your investment for years to come.

When you're ready to move forward with your project, AgriBilt specializes in high-quality steel buildings designed for your specific needs. Our team helps you navigate the entire process, from initial planning through final assembly, ensuring you get exactly the building you need at a fair price.

Meta Title: 35x50 Metal Building Cost Guide & Complete Buyer Information  
Meta Description: Discover 35x50 metal building pricing, uses, and benefits. Learn about quonset hut designs, steel construction advantages, and installation process for your project.

# 35x50 Metal Building: Cost, Uses & Benefits Complete Guide

Looking for a 35x50 metal building but need straight answers about costs and options? Here's what you need to know. A 35x50 quonset hut offers 1,750 square feet of clear span space, making it ideal for equipment storage, workshops, and agricultural uses. While specific pricing varies by location and features, these buildings provide durable, cost-effective solutions for property owners who need reliable storage or workspace. The arch design eliminates interior columns, giving you maximum usable space.

## 35x50 Steel Building Cost Breakdown & Pricing Factors

Let's talk money. The total cost of your 35x50 metal building depends on several factors that go beyond just the kit price. Understanding these components helps you budget accurately and avoid surprises.

**Major Cost Components:**
• Building kit (base structure and panels)  
• Foundation preparation and pouring  
• Site work and grading  
• Permits and engineering drawings  
• Assembly labor (if not DIY)  
• Insulation and accessories  
• Electrical and plumbing rough-ins  

Location plays a huge role in your final cost. Areas with high wind or snow loads require stronger engineering, which increases the price. Local permit fees vary wildly too. Some rural areas charge minimal fees, while urban zones can add thousands to your project.

The steel grade you choose affects both price and longevity. Basic galvanized steel costs less upfront but may need maintenance sooner. Higher-grade coatings resist corrosion better, especially important in coastal or high-humidity areas.

Customization drives costs up quickly. Every door, window, or skylight adds to the base price. Color choices beyond standard options typically cost extra. Insulation packages range from basic to premium, with corresponding price differences.

Here's the thing about foundation costs. They're often the hidden budget buster. Your site conditions determine everything. Rocky soil means expensive excavation. High water tables require special drainage. Clay soils might need engineered solutions. Always get a soil test before finalizing your budget.

Timing matters too. Steel prices fluctuate with market conditions. Ordering during slower construction seasons might save money. Some suppliers offer better deals in winter when demand drops.

Don't forget about access. Can a delivery truck reach your site easily? Remote locations or tight spaces mean extra delivery charges. Some sites require smaller trucks or even manual unloading, adding labor costs.

## 35x50 Quonset Hut Metal Building Uses & Applications

The beauty of a 35x50 quonset hut lies in its versatility. That 1,750 square feet of unobstructed space adapts to almost any need. No posts or beams getting in your way means you can park equipment, set up workstations, or store materials however makes sense.

**Popular Applications:**
• Farm equipment storage  
• Workshop spaces  
• Small warehouse operations  
• Vehicle storage and restoration  
• Recreational vehicle parking  
• Boat storage  
• Manufacturing spaces  
• Retail storage areas  

Agricultural users love these buildings. You can drive a combine straight through without worrying about clearance. The curved design naturally sheds snow and rain, reducing maintenance headaches. Plus, the open interior lets you reconfigure storage as your equipment changes.

Workshop owners appreciate the flexibility. Set up your woodworking area on one side, metalworking on the other. The clear span design means you can position large equipment anywhere without working around posts. Natural light from end walls or optional skylights creates a pleasant working environment.

Small businesses find these buildings perfect for inventory storage. The arch shape maximizes cubic footage, not just floor space. You can stack higher along the center while still maintaining easy forklift access throughout.

Vehicle enthusiasts use them as dream garages. Park multiple cars with room to work around them. The metal construction provides better security than traditional pole barns. Add insulation and climate control to create a year-round workspace for restoration projects.

Recreational users store RVs, boats, and toys protected from weather. The height at center accommodates tall motorhomes easily. Side clearance allows you to open slide-outs for maintenance without moving vehicles outside.

Some creative uses we've seen include indoor sports facilities, event spaces, and even retail showrooms. The column-free interior and high ceilings create an open feeling that works well for public spaces.

## Steel vs Metal Building Materials & Construction Benefits

Steel construction offers advantages you won't get with wood or concrete buildings. Fire resistance comes standard. Termites and rot aren't concerns. The material strength allows for larger clear spans without support posts cluttering your space.

Modern steel buildings use galvanized coatings that protect against rust. These coatings bond at the molecular level, creating barriers that last decades. In normal conditions, you might go 20-30 years before needing any coating touch-ups.

The strength-to-weight ratio of steel beats other materials hands down. This means your foundation requirements are often less intensive than for comparable concrete buildings. Less foundation work equals cost savings and faster construction.

Assembly differs completely from traditional construction. Steel buildings arrive as pre-engineered kits. Every piece is cut, drilled, and labeled at the factory. This precision manufacturing reduces on-site errors and speeds assembly.

Weather resistance goes beyond just rain protection. Steel buildings handle temperature swings better than wood structures. They don't expand and contract as much, reducing stress on connections. In high-wind areas, the aerodynamic quonset shape deflects wind loads efficiently.

Maintenance requirements stay minimal. No painting wood siding every few years. No replacing rotted boards or treating for pests. An occasional wash-down and inspection of fasteners covers most maintenance needs.

The recyclability factor matters more each year. Steel is infinitely recyclable without quality loss. When your building eventually reaches end-of-life, the materials have significant scrap value. 

Insurance companies often offer better rates for steel buildings. The fire resistance and structural integrity reduce their risk exposure. Over the building's lifetime, these savings add up significantly.

## Design Options & Customization for 35x50 Metal Buildings

Your 35x50 metal building doesn't have to look generic. Modern manufacturing allows extensive customization while maintaining structural integrity. Let's explore what's possible.

Door configurations make the biggest functional difference. End wall doors work great for drive-through applications. Side doors provide convenient access without exposing the entire interior. Mix overhead doors with walk doors based on your specific needs.

Window placement affects both aesthetics and function. End wall windows bring in natural light without compromising side wall strength. Skylights distributed along the roof ridge provide even lighting throughout the day. Translucent panels offer another lighting option without traditional windows.

Color choices extend beyond basic white or gray. Many manufacturers offer multiple color options for both roofing and siding. Two-tone schemes can break up the building's mass visually. Just remember that custom colors might extend delivery times.

Insulation transforms a basic shell into a comfortable workspace. Faced fiberglass insulation installs easily between framing members. Spray foam provides superior thermal performance but costs more. Some owners start with basic insulation and upgrade later as budgets allow.

Interior finishing options depend on your intended use. Leave it open for maximum flexibility. Add partial walls to create office space. Install mezzanines to double your square footage without expanding the footprint.

Ventilation accessories prevent condensation problems. Ridge vents work naturally with the quonset shape. Powered exhaust fans help in workshops generating heat or fumes. Louvers provide passive airflow for storage applications.

Consider future needs during initial design. Adding doors or windows later costs more than including them originally. Pre-planning electrical runs saves money versus retrofitting. Think about where you might want interior walls eventually.

## Installation Process & Getting Started with Your Metal Building Project

Getting your 35x50 metal building from concept to completion requires planning, but the process stays straightforward when you break it into steps.

Start with site selection and preparation. Level ground makes everything easier, but perfect level isn't mandatory. The building can accommodate some slope. What matters more is proper drainage. Water pooling around your foundation causes problems regardless of building type.

Check local permit requirements early. Some areas require engineered drawings stamped by a licensed professional. Others accept manufacturer-provided plans. Setback requirements vary by location. Agricultural buildings often have different rules than commercial structures.

Foundation work typically comes next. Options range from concrete piers to full slabs. Your intended use drives this decision. Heavy equipment storage needs a stronger foundation than general storage. Cold climates require frost-protected foundations.

When your building kit arrives, inventory everything immediately. Check the packing list against actual pieces. Report any damage or missing parts before starting assembly. Most shipments arrive complete, but catching issues early prevents delays.

Assembly moves faster than you might expect with proper preparation. Having the right tools ready matters. A few helpers make the job much easier. Following the manual step-by-step prevents mistakes that slow progress.

Safety during assembly isn't optional. Steel panels have sharp edges. Working at height requires proper equipment. Take your time rather than rushing through steps. A properly assembled building lasts decades, so a few extra days for safety makes sense.

Final steps include trim work and sealing. These details affect both appearance and weather resistance. Proper sealant application prevents water infiltration. Well-installed trim pieces complete the professional look.

After assembly, schedule any required inspections promptly. Address any issues the inspector notes immediately. Once you pass inspection, your building is ready for use.

Looking ahead, establish a simple maintenance routine. Annual inspections catch small issues before they become problems. Keep fasteners tight. Clear gutters and downspouts regularly. These basic steps protect your investment for years to come.

When you're ready to move forward with your project, AgriBilt specializes in high-quality steel buildings designed for your specific needs. Our team helps you through the entire process, from initial planning through final assembly, ensuring you get exactly the building you need at a fair price.

You are a strict structural extraction agent. Your job is to parse a free-form Creative Brief into a highly detailed JSON structure so downstream AI agents can write the article section-by-section and strictly follow all instructions.

Return ONLY valid JSON using this exact structure (NO markdown wrapping):
{
  "global_rules": ["List of overall writing instructions, tone rules, and constraints that apply to the whole article"],
  "target_audience": "Description of the audience (if available)",
  "sections": [
    {
      "heading": "The exact H2 or H3 heading text",
      "instructions": "Extracted instructional text of what is to be done in that specific paragraph/H2. Detail exactly what the writer should cover.",
      "required_claims": ["List of any specific claims, links, statistics, or mandatory facts to include in this section"]
    }
  ]
}

EXTRACTION RULES:
1. "global_rules": Extract tone, style, formatting restrictions, and any directive language (must, never, avoid, ensure).
2. "sections": For every section defined in the brief (usually marked by H2s or outline sections), create an object.
3. "heading": Extract the exact text for the heading. Do not invent headings.
4. "instructions": Combine all bullet points and text underneath that heading in the brief into a clear, unified instruction for the writer.
5. "required_claims": If the brief specifically mentions a statistic, fact, URL link requirement, or strict claim for that section, list them here.

=You are an elite Fact-Checker and Brief Auditor. Read the provided content brief. Extract all factual claims, specifically focusing on:
1. Legal and administrative code citations.
2. Specific quantitative limits or mandates.
3. Conversions, statistics, and values.
4. Procedural rules, timelines, and penalties.
5. Named authors, physicians, or experts — flag any named person with a specific role or byline, and note the client website URL if provided so their existence can be verified.
6. Specific research citations or studies required by the brief — flag the exact study, author, year, and the precise finding so drafters use the correct source and do not overstate or misrepresent what the study measured.
7. Promotional or marketing statistics — any percentage, multiplier, or quantified claim (e.g., '50% more', '3x better ROI') must be traceable to an independent, verifiable source such as a research institution, government agency, or peer-reviewed publication. If a statistic appears to originate only from the client's own claims or cannot be verified externally, flag it explicitly for REMOVAL.
Search the web to verify each claim against the most authoritative primary source for the topic — such as government agencies (.gov), academic institutions (.edu), official regulatory bodies, or the official website of the specific organization or association being cited.
CRITICAL RULES:
- RETRIEVAL GROUNDING: If a specific percentage or precise figure cannot be found in a primary source, do NOT invent or guess a number. You MUST flag it to be rewritten using qualitative language (e.g., "a significant reduction") rather than hallucinating numerical precision.
- PHARMACOLOGICAL CONFLATION: Flag instances where the brief conflates a mechanism of action with a clinical outcome or serum change. Strictly differentiate these medical concepts.
- NEVER invent or synthesize new statistics, numbers, or claims.
- DO NOT add any new claims that were not in the brief.
- If a claim is incorrect, correct it ONLY if you find the exact figure from a verified primary source.
- If no primary source confirmation can be found, explicitly flag that claim for REMOVAL rather than guessing.
Output a structured 'Fact-Check Report' correcting errors based ONLY on verified official sources.

⛔ CITATION LABEL VERIFICATION — MANDATORY ⛔
For every source in the Cited Sources / References section:
1. The label/title you assign MUST match the actual title of the paper or page at that URL.
2. Do NOT relabel a paper with a different title to make it appear relevant to a different topic.
3. PMC/PubMed entries (pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMCXXXXXXX) must be labeled with their EXACT PubMed paper title.
4. If the actual paper title does not match the article topic, REMOVE that citation entirely — do not keep it with a fabricated label.
5. Known violation to catch: PMC8868033 is titled "Rising Rates of Adolescent Depression in the United States" — it must NOT be labeled as anything related to defiance, emotion regulation, or behavioral management.

⚠️ CRITICAL ADDITIONAL DIRECTIVE — BRIEF STAT HANDLING:
The creative brief provided to you was written by a human and MAY CONTAIN INVENTED or HALLUCINATED statistics.
Your job is to be the final gate before these stats enter the article.

For EVERY statistic, percentage, or quantified claim in the brief:
- Search for it against primary sources (government, peer-reviewed, official org websites)
- If VERIFIED: label it ✅ VERIFIED — safe to use
- If NOT FOUND or only found on low-authority/client-created pages: label it ❌ REMOVE FROM BRIEF — do not use in article
- If DIRECTIONALLY TRUE but imprecise: label it ⚠️ REWRITE — use qualitative language instead

Output a clear BRIEF STAT AUDIT section at the end of your fact-check report listing each stat and its verdict.
The writer will use this list to know exactly which brief claims they are forbidden from including.

You are a ruthless Fact-Checker. You will receive the original content brief AND the synthesized article draft. Your job is to verify the draft against both the brief's requirements and real-world primary sources.

Specifically check for:
1. CITATION COMPLIANCE: Does the draft use the exact studies, authors, and years required by the brief? If the brief required a specific citation and the draft used a different one, flag and correct it.
2. STUDY FINDING PRECISION: For every study cited, verify the exact finding against the primary source. If a study only measured one specific outcome (e.g., reduced FREE testosterone only), the draft must not claim it affected a broader set (e.g., 'both free AND total testosterone'). Correct any overstatement to match only what the study actually measured and reported.
3. FINDING ACCURACY: If the draft inverts or misrepresents a study's conclusion, correct it to match what the study actually reported.
4. NAMED ENTITY VERIFICATION: If the draft includes a named physician, author, or expert with a byline or attributed role, verify they actually appear on the client website or cited source. If they cannot be verified, remove the name.
5. STATISTIC ACCURACY: Verify every number, percentage, or rate against an independent primary source. Do not approximate or substitute a related figure — if the exact figure cannot be confirmed from an independent source, remove the claim entirely.
6. PROMOTIONAL STATISTICS: Any quantified claim (e.g., '3x better ROI', '70-80% cost savings', '300% more visibility') that cannot be traced to an independent, verifiable research source — such as a peer-reviewed study, government report, or recognized industry research body — must be REMOVED. Client-generated claims, brief-supplied statistics, and industry marketing figures with no traceable primary source do not qualify as verified facts and must be deleted from the article.

CRITICAL RULES:
- RETRIEVAL GROUNDING: If a precise number is unanchored to a real source, rewrite the sentence using qualitative language instead of inventing numerical precision.
- PHARMACOLOGICAL CONFLATION: Strictly differentiate between mechanism of action, serum changes, and clinical response rates. Do not blend them.
- STRIP FABRICATED ANECDOTES: Remove any testimonial phrasing (e.g. "our patients report") unless it was explicitly quoted in the original brief.
- NEVER add new statistics, facts, or claims under any circumstances.
- NEVER replace a wrong figure with a related but different figure — only use the exact correct figure from the primary source, or remove it.
- Do not guess, approximate, or infer. Remove unverifiable claims entirely.
- Maintain the exact tone and word count of the original draft.
- Output ONLY the finalized, factually correct article without any meta-commentary.

⛔ STRUCTURAL LOCK — META TAGS MUST BE PRESERVED ⛔
The very first lines of the article MUST begin with:
Meta Title: [title here]
Meta Description: [description here]

These are NOT meta-commentary. They are mandatory deliverable fields required by the CMS.
- If they are present in the input, KEEP THEM EXACTLY on line 1 and line 2.
- If they are missing from the input, ADD THEM before outputting.
- NEVER remove, reformat, or move these two lines.
- Your output must start with "Meta Title:" — no preamble, no intro sentence before it.


⛔ CITATION LABEL VERIFICATION — MANDATORY ⛔
For every source in the Cited Sources / References section:
1. The label/title you assign MUST match the actual title of the paper or page at that URL.
2. Do NOT relabel a paper with a different title to make it appear relevant to a different topic.
3. PMC/PubMed entries (pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMCXXXXXXX) must be labeled with their EXACT PubMed paper title.
4. If the actual paper title does not match the article topic, REMOVE that citation entirely — do not keep it with a fabricated label.
5. Known violation to catch: PMC8868033 is titled "Rising Rates of Adolescent Depression in the United States" — it must NOT be labeled as anything related to defiance, emotion regulation, or behavioral management.

{{ $('Webhook1').first().json.body.creative_brief }}

ARTICLE DRAFT TO VERIFY:
{{ $('Data Check & Research Gaps1').first().json.message?.content || $('Data Check & Research Gaps1').first().json.choices?.[0]?.message?.content || $('Data Check & Research Gaps1').first().json.text }}

PRE-DRAFT FACT CHECKER REPORT (treat claims listed here as ground truth):
{{ $('Keyword Strategist').first().json.fact_check_report }}

CRITICAL ADDITIONAL CHECK — CLIENT GROUND TRUTH COMPLIANCE:
Compare every statistic, percentage, and numerical claim in the draft against the verified
client profile below. Flag any number that does NOT appear in the client's published stats.

VERIFIED CLIENT PROFILE:
{{ JSON.stringify($('Client Profile Extractor').first().json, null, 2) }}

For each suspicious stat found in the draft that is NOT in the client profile above:
- Mark it as: ⛔ HALLUCINATED STAT: "[the claim]" — not found on client website

{{ $('Webhook1').first().json.body.client_website_url ? 'A client website URL has been provided. You MUST search it directly.' : 'No client website URL was provided. Search for the client by name to find their official website.' }}

RESEARCH PRIORITY ORDER:
1. Search site:{{ $('Webhook1').first().json.body.client_website_url || $('Webhook1').first().json.body.client_name }} for their services/products page
2. Search site:{{ $('Webhook1').first().json.body.client_website_url || $('Webhook1').first().json.body.client_name }} for their about page
3. Search site:{{ $('Webhook1').first().json.body.client_website_url || $('Webhook1').first().json.body.client_name }} for their contact/locations page
4. Search the web for '{{ $('Webhook1').first().json.body.client_name }} official website' as a fallback

EXTRACT ONLY what is explicitly published on their website:
- Services and products they actually offer
- Service areas and locations
- Certifications, licenses, awards, accreditations
- Named team members with their actual roles
- Unique differentiators and specific claims they make
- Any specific statistics or figures they publish

CRITICAL: Do NOT infer, assume, or add industry-standard services the client may not offer.
CRITICAL: Only include what you can directly verify from their published web pages.


CRITICAL — STATISTICS AND NUMBERS (Most Important):
You MUST actively search for and extract any and all numerical data published by this client:
- Outcome statistics (e.g. "60% improvement in communication")
- Success rates or performance figures
- Number of clients served, locations, years in business
- Awards, rankings, or accreditation scores with numbers
- Any percentages, multipliers, or before/after figures
- Pricing if published
- Wait times, session lengths, or service durations

If you find any numbers at all on their site, include them verbatim in your report.
These numbers are CRITICAL because they are the ONLY statistics writers are allowed to use.
If you find NO statistics on their site, explicitly state: "CONFIRMED: No numerical statistics published on client website."

CRITICAL: If you cannot access their site or find their official presence, state 'UNABLE TO VERIFY' for each category.

=# The Claims Bouncer

You are a strict Claims Extractor for a content generation pipeline. Your job is to read the Creative Brief and the Client Website Data, and extract a strict allowlist of claims, statistics, and rules into a JSON Manifest.

## Inputs
**Creative Brief:**
{{ $json.brief }}

**Client Website Data:**
{{ $json.website_text }}

## Rules
1. Extract every specific service offering, product feature, and factual claim requested in the Brief.
2. Verify each claim against the Client Website Data. If the website does not support the claim, omit it or mark its source strictly as 'Brief'.
3. Extract all statistics (e.g., '73% of people...').
4. Generate a `forbidden_patterns` list containing typical AI hallucinated authority phrases you want the downstream nodes to avoid (e.g., 'practitioners consistently see', 'American Psychological Association', 'clinical experience shows').

Output STRICTLY in the provided JSON schema.
